h1Title: Bunion (Hallux Valgus): Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: A bunion (hallux valgus) is a progressive bony deformity at the first metatarsophalangeal joint in which the big toe drifts toward the second toe and the metatarsal head protrudes medially, forming a visible bony bump. Bunions affect approximately 23% of adults aged 18-65 and 35% of adults over 65 globally, making them one of the most prevalent forefoot conditions managed in podiatric practice. Women are affected roughly four times more often than men, largely due to footwear choices. Pain arises from direct pressure on the prominent joint and from secondary metatarsalgia as the first ray loses load-bearing function. Conservative treatment with wide footwear and orthotics controls symptoms in most patients; corrective surgery (osteotomy or arthrodesis) achieves satisfactory outcomes in over 85% of symptomatic cases unresponsive to nonsurgical management.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 23% of adults aged 18-65 and 35% of adults over 65 have hallux valgus (Nix et al. 2010, systematic review of 78 studies)
keyStats.demographics: Women affected 2-4× more frequently than men; prevalence rises markedly after age 50; more common in populations that routinely wear shoes
keyStats.avgAge: Symptom onset most commonly in the fourth to sixth decade; juvenile hallux valgus presents before skeletal maturity (under age 18)
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 250-300 million people affected worldwide based on global prevalence data (Nix et al. 2010)

definition: Hallux valgus (ICD-10: M20.1) is a triplanar deformity of the first ray characterized by lateral deviation of the great toe at the first metatarsophalangeal (MTP) joint, medial deviation of the first metatarsal head, and pronation of the hallux around its long axis. The hallux valgus angle (HVA) — the angle between the first metatarsal and proximal phalanx on weight-bearing radiograph — defines severity: mild deformity is HVA 15-20°, moderate is 20-40°, and severe is above 40°. The intermetatarsal angle (IMA) between the first and second metatarsals also increases as the deformity progresses. The medially prominent first metatarsal head forms the bunion bump, which becomes inflamed from repetitive shoe pressure. Soft-tissue contractures develop over time: the adductor hallucis muscle and lateral joint capsule tighten, maintaining the deformity, while the medial capsule stretches. Sesamoid bones within the flexor hallucis brevis tendon migrate laterally, further destabilizing the joint. The first MTP joint eventually undergoes arthritic change in severe deformity. Podiatrists, orthopedic foot and ankle surgeons, and physical therapists all contribute to management, with podiatrists typically coordinating conservative care.

typesClassification.description: Minimal metatarsal head prominence; pain only with tight footwear. Responds well to footwear modification, orthotics, and toe spacers. Surgery rarely required.

Visible bunion deformity; bursa may form over the medial eminence. Toe crowding may cause second-toe hammering. Conservative treatment effective in many; distal metatarsal osteotomy considered if pain persists.

Gross toe deviation, often overlapping the second toe. Significant MTP joint subluxation or arthritic change. Surgical correction typically required; proximal osteotomy or first MTP arthrodesis are primary options.

Deformity presenting before skeletal maturity with open growth plates. More likely to be bilateral, hypermobile, and familial. Surgical correction deferred until skeletal maturity if possible because of high recurrence with early surgery.

Advanced stage where cartilage loss, joint-space narrowing, and osteophyte formation accompany the deformity. First MTP arthrodesis provides definitive pain relief with sacrifice of joint motion; total joint replacement is an alternative in older, low-demand patients.

primarySymptoms: Pain and tenderness directly over the medial bunion prominence, worsened by wearing closed or narrow footwear that compresses the metatarsal head.
Visible bony bump on the inner aspect of the foot at the base of the big toe, often with overlying redness, warmth, and swollen bursa (bursitis).
Gradual lateral drift of the great toe toward the second toe, eventually causing the big toe to overlap or underlap the adjacent digit.
Restricted range of motion at the first MTP joint — particularly loss of dorsiflexion — causing a stiff, painful push-off gait.
Callus and pain on the plantar surface beneath the second and third metatarsal heads (transfer metatarsalgia) as the first ray loses its normal weight-bearing role.
Second-toe deformity (hammer toe or crossover toe) arising from crowding and destabilization by the laterally drifting hallux.
Burning or aching pain at the end of the day after prolonged standing or walking, relieved by rest and elevation.
Difficulty fitting standard-width footwear, requiring wider or specially modified shoes.
Numbness or tingling over the dorsal medial aspect of the big toe from pressure on the dorsal digital nerve as it crosses the bunion prominence.
earlyWarningSigns: Mild medial MTP joint prominence that becomes tender only in narrow or heeled footwear — the earliest clinical sign, present before significant angular deformity.
A widening of the forefoot noticed when purchasing shoes, reflecting increase in the intermetatarsal angle.
Morning stiffness at the first MTP joint lasting less than 30 minutes that resolves with gentle walking.
First-degree family history of bunions combined with flexible flat feet or hypermobility — a population at high risk for early intervention.
emergencySigns: Acute severe pain, swelling, and warmth at the MTP joint with systemic fever — must exclude septic arthritis or acute gout flare, both of which require urgent evaluation.
Sudden loss of great-toe sensation with pallor or cyanosis suggesting neurovascular compromise from acute bursal swelling or shoe-related nerve compression.
Skin ulceration over the bunion prominence in patients with diabetes mellitus or peripheral arterial disease — risk of deep-space infection and osteomyelitis.

causes.description: Inherited hypermobility of the first tarsometatarsal joint, pes planus (flat foot), and a round first metatarsal head all predispose to hallux valgus. Family history is present in approximately 68% of patients according to meta-analysis data.

Shoes that force the forefoot into a tapered toe box apply continuous valgus force to the hallux and constrain the metatarsal heads together. High heels shift weight anteriorly, amplifying forefoot loading. This explains the 2-4× female-to-male prevalence ratio in shod populations versus near-equal prevalence in unshod populations.

Excessive pronation of the subtalar joint causes medial deviation of the first metatarsal (metatarsus primus varus) and unlocks the first tarsometatarsal joint, making hallux valgus angulation more likely. Studies show hallux valgus is 2× more common in people with clinical flat feet.
Excessive dorsiflexion laxity at the first tarsometatarsal joint (Lapidus joint) allows the first metatarsal to splay medially with weight-bearing, the initial step in deformity progression.

Synovitis of the first MTP joint destroys the supporting ligament complex and causes progressive joint subluxation, producing hallux valgus as a secondary feature of systemic disease in roughly 40% of rheumatoid arthritis patients.

riskFactors.description: Women are 2-4× more likely to develop bunions than men, attributed to higher rates of pointed-toe and high-heeled footwear use and, in part, to ligamentous laxity differences.
First-degree relative with bunions increases personal risk approximately 2.9× (Pique-Vidal et al. 2007). Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern with variable penetrance.
Flatfoot deformity is present in up to 50% of hallux valgus patients presenting for surgical evaluation. Untreated pes planus allows ongoing first-ray instability and bunion progression.
Switching from narrow to wide-toe-box footwear reduces daily compressive force on the bunion and slows progression; population studies show unshod populations have much lower bunion prevalence.
MTP synovitis and joint destruction in rheumatoid arthritis produce hallux valgus in 30-50% of patients. Disease activity control reduces progression rate.
Ligament laxity increases and MTP joint cartilage thins with age. Prevalence rises from 15% under age 40 to over 35% after age 65 (Nix et al. 2010).

riskFactors.factor: Hypermobile first ray or generalized joint hypermobility
riskFactors.description: Beighton score ≥4 (generalized hypermobility) is associated with early-onset and more severe hallux valgus progression.

affectedDemographics: Women represent 65-75% of patients presenting for bunion treatment in primary care and surgical referral series worldwide.
Prevalence doubles from 15% in those under 40 to over 35% in adults over 65, reflecting cumulative footwear exposure and ligament laxity with age (Nix et al. 2010).
Juvenile hallux valgus (onset before age 18) accounts for 3-6% of all cases and is more commonly bilateral, familial, and associated with generalized joint hypermobility.
Higher prevalence in Asian populations compared with European populations in some studies, possibly reflecting footwear styles and foot morphology differences.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is clinical and radiographic. The podiatrist or foot and ankle surgeon begins with a structured history: onset and progression, shoe types worn, occupation, and family history. Physical examination records the hallux valgus angle visually, tests first MTP joint range of motion (normal dorsiflexion 65-75°), assesses the first tarsometatarsal joint for hypermobility, and evaluates the subtalar joint for associated pes planus. Callosity distribution on the plantar forefoot indicates transfer loading. Weight-bearing anteroposterior, lateral, and sesamoid-axial radiographs are the diagnostic standard: the hallux valgus angle (HVA) is measured between the axes of the first metatarsal and proximal phalanx; an IMA greater than 9° between the first and second metatarsals confirms metatarsus primus varus. Joint-space width assesses concurrent arthritic change. Sesamoid position is graded on the axial view — lateral displacement predicts joint instability. Mild deformity (HVA 15-20°) with manageable symptoms is treated conservatively. Moderate-to-severe deformity (HVA >20° with pain unresponsive to conservative care) warrants surgical consultation. MRI is not routinely required but may be obtained if plantar plate tear or avascular necrosis of the metatarsal head is suspected.

diagnosticTests.test: Weight-bearing foot radiograph (AP and lateral views)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies hallux valgus angle, intermetatarsal angle, sesamoid position, and arthritic change — the key measures guiding surgical planning
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: X-ray taken while standing on the foot (weight-bearing). Painless. Two views take less than 5 minutes. Results available immediately.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Grades lateral sesamoid displacement, which indicates first MTP joint instability and determines osteotomy type for surgery
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A third X-ray view taken with the foot in a specific position. Painless, completed within the same appointment as the AP and lateral views.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies first MTP dorsiflexion deficit and differentiates hallux valgus from hallux rigidus (osteoarthritis of the MTP joint)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician passively moves the big toe and measures the arc with a goniometer. Takes 2-3 minutes; may cause mild discomfort at the end of range.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps forefoot load distribution, confirming transfer metatarsalgia and guiding orthotic prescription
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient walks barefoot over a pressure plate or wears in-shoe pressure sensors. Takes 5-10 minutes; completely painless.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies first-ray instability requiring Lapidus arthrodesis rather than distal osteotomy at surgery
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician stabilizes the lesser metatarsals and moves the first metatarsal up and down. Painless; takes about one minute.

treatmentOverview: Management follows a graduated approach from conservative to surgical. Conservative treatment is first-line for all patients and can control symptoms in the majority who do not have severe deformity or significant arthritic change. Footwear modification to a wide, low-heeled, soft-toed shoe removes the primary mechanical stressor; this single change provides meaningful relief in 50-60% of patients. Toe spacers and bunion splints worn at night reduce pressure and may slow progression in mild cases, although they do not reverse the bony deformity. Custom foot orthotics address pes planus and first-ray hypermobility, redistributing plantar load. Padded bunion sleeves protect the prominent medial eminence from shoe friction. Anti-inflammatory analgesia (NSAIDs) and ice manage acute flares. Physiotherapy targeting intrinsic foot-muscle strengthening, particularly of abductor hallucis, improves dynamic first-ray stability. Corticosteroid injection into the first MTP joint or overlying bursa provides short-term relief (2-4 months) in bursitis-dominant presentations. When these measures fail to provide adequate function and quality of life after 3-6 months, or when deformity is moderate-to-severe at presentation, surgical correction is appropriate. Over 130 surgical techniques exist; the most widely performed are the Chevron (Austin) distal metatarsal osteotomy for mild-moderate deformity and proximal metatarsal osteotomy or Lapidus tarsometatarsal arthrodesis for severe deformity or hypermobile first ray. First MTP arthrodesis is preferred for severe arthritic change. Hallux valgus correction does not guarantee prevention of recurrence; footwear modification and orthotic use must continue postoperatively.

medicalTreatments.name: Wide toe-box, low-heeled footwear
medicalTreatments.description: Removing constrictive footwear is the single most effective conservative intervention. Shoes with a toe box at least as wide as the metatarsal heads eliminate direct bunion compression. Footwear with heel height under 2.5 cm reduces anterior weight shift.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom relief in 50-60% of patients with mild-moderate bunion; does not reverse deformity but halts pressure-related progression

medicalTreatments.name: Custom foot orthotics
medicalTreatments.description: Prefabricated or custom-cast insoles with medial arch support and first-ray cutouts redistribute plantar pressure and address the biomechanical drivers (pronation, first-ray hypermobility). Combined with footwear change in most conservative protocols.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces forefoot loading and improves pain scores by 20-30% versus footwear change alone in RCT evidence

medicalTreatments.name: Toe spacers and night splints
medicalTreatments.description: Soft silicone spacers between the first and second toe reduce dynamic valgus force during walking. Night splints hold the hallux in corrected position during sleep. Neither device corrects established bony deformity but both improve symptom comfort.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest pain reduction (20-30% VAS improvement); no evidence of angular deformity correction in RCTs

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily; naproxen 500 mg twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral NSAIDs reduce MTP joint inflammation and bursitis-related pain during flares. Used for short courses (7-14 days) given GI and cardiovascular risks of long-term use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for acute pain flares; no effect on deformity progression

medicalTreatments.name: Corticosteroid injection (methylprednisolone 40 mg with local anaesthetic)
medicalTreatments.description: Injection into the first MTP joint or overlying bursa reduces acute inflammatory pain. Technique: medial approach to the joint, avoiding the flexor tendons.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction lasting 6-12 weeks in approximately 65% of patients; repeated injections risk tendon weakening and accelerate cartilage damage

surgicalOptions.name: Chevron (Austin) distal metatarsal osteotomy
surgicalOptions.description: V-shaped bone cut at the first metatarsal neck, allowing lateral translation of the metatarsal head. Fixated with one or two screws or pins. Suitable for mild-to-moderate hallux valgus (HVA 15-35°, IMA under 16°). Most commonly performed bunion operation worldwide.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Patient satisfaction 85-95%; mean correction of HVA by 15-17°; radiographic recurrence in 10-15% at 5 years

surgicalOptions.name: Lapidus tarsometatarsal arthrodesis
surgicalOptions.description: Fusion of the first tarsometatarsal joint corrects deformity at its root — first-ray hypermobility — and achieves greater angular correction than distal osteotomy. Preferred for severe deformity (HVA >40°) or hypermobile first ray. Requires 6-8 weeks non-weight-bearing.
surgicalOptions.successRate: HVA correction of 20-25°; union rate 95-98% with modern locking-plate fixation; patient satisfaction above 90% at 2 years

surgicalOptions.name: First metatarsophalangeal joint arthrodesis
surgicalOptions.description: Fusion of the first MTP joint in a functionally optimal position (15-20° dorsiflexion, 10-15° valgus). Eliminates painful joint motion and provides permanent deformity correction. First choice for severe arthritic change or revision surgery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Union rate 95-97%; long-term patient satisfaction 85-90%; requires permanent adaptation of footwear to a rocker-bottom sole

surgicalOptions.name: Minimally invasive (percutaneous) Chevron and Akin osteotomy (MICA)
surgicalOptions.description: Burr-based osteotomies performed through 2-4 mm skin portals under fluoroscopic guidance. Combines distal metatarsal osteotomy with Akin proximal phalanx closing wedge osteotomy. Smaller scars, faster return to footwear than open technique.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Equivalent radiographic correction to open Chevron in RCTs; return to normal footwear at 6-8 weeks in most series

alternativeTreatments.name: Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injection for MTP synovitis
alternativeTreatments.description: Small pilot studies show reduction in synovitis and pain scores at 3 months compared with corticosteroid in hallux valgus-associated MTP synovitis. Evidence insufficient for routine recommendation; may be considered before surgical referral in selected patients.

alternativeTreatments.name: Intrinsic foot strengthening with toe-spread exercises
alternativeTreatments.description: Exercises activating abductor hallucis (towel scrunches, toe-spread and hold, short-foot exercise) have RCT evidence for improving first MTP joint alignment by 3-5° and reducing pain in mild hallux valgus over 8 weeks.

whySeeSpecialist: A podiatrist or foot and ankle surgeon should evaluate any bunion causing daily pain, restricting footwear choice, causing second-toe deformity, or showing progressive angular deformity on serial radiographs. Early podiatric assessment guides orthotic prescription and footwear advice before deformity becomes severe enough to require complex reconstruction.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a podiatric surgeon or orthopedic foot and ankle surgeon with experience in forefoot reconstruction who performs at least 50-100 bunion corrections annually. Confirm they offer minimally invasive techniques alongside open procedures and have an in-house gait laboratory or orthotic service for conservative management.
hospitalCriteria: Foot and ankle surgical unit with weight-bearing fluoroscopy capability for intraoperative correction assessment
Podiatry outpatient service with orthotic fabrication and gait analysis
Day-surgery capability for elective forefoot procedures
Post-surgical physiotherapy and footwear rehabilitation service
preventionStrategies: Wear wide-toe-box, low-heeled (under 2.5 cm) footwear from early adulthood, particularly if there is a family history of bunions.
Fit custom orthotics if flat feet or first-ray hypermobility are identified — these address the biomechanical predisposing factors.
Maintain healthy body weight to reduce forefoot loading; each kilogram of body weight generates approximately 2-3 kg of ground reaction force at the forefoot.
Perform regular intrinsic foot-strengthening exercises (towel scrunches, toe-spreading, short-foot) to support the first ray dynamically.
Have footwear professionally fitted, particularly in adolescence — this period of skeletal plasticity is the most critical window for prevention.
lifestyleModifications: Replace all narrow, pointed, or high-heeled shoes with wide-toe-box footwear immediately on diagnosis.
Wear prescribed orthotics in all shoes, including sports footwear and casual footwear, not just work shoes.
Apply a silicone toe spacer between the first and second toes during walking to reduce dynamic valgus force.
Elevate the foot and apply ice (15-20 minutes) after prolonged standing to reduce bursal inflammation.
Perform daily abductor hallucis strengthening exercises (seated toe-spread and hold, 3 sets of 10) to slow deformity progression.
Reduce prolonged standing time on hard floors if possible; anti-fatigue matting and regular sitting breaks reduce forefoot loading.

recommended: Anti-inflammatory foods (oily fish, nuts, olive oil, berries) to reduce systemic and joint inflammation
Adequate calcium and vitamin D to support bone health, particularly before and after surgical correction
Lean protein sources to support soft tissue healing post-surgery
Hydration of at least 2 L per day to maintain joint synovial fluid quality
avoid: Excess sodium, which promotes edema and increases postoperative foot swelling
High-purine foods if concurrent gout is present, which can cause acute MTP flares mistaken for bunion pain
Alcohol during postoperative recovery, which impairs bone and soft-tissue healing
Excessive sugar intake, associated with systemic inflammation and impaired wound healing

exerciseGuidelines: Low-impact exercise (swimming, cycling, elliptical trainer) maintains fitness without high forefoot loading. Running and jumping sports with hard footwear worsen pain during active flares. After surgical correction, weight-bearing resumes per surgeon protocol (typically 2-6 weeks in a protective boot), with return to sports at 3-6 months for simple osteotomy and 6-12 months after arthrodesis.
dailyManagement: Wear prescribed wide-toe-box footwear throughout the day; remove narrow shoes even for brief errands.
Insert orthotics and toe spacers before putting on footwear each morning.
Ice the bunion for 15-20 minutes after activities involving prolonged standing or walking.
Complete the daily abductor hallucis strengthening routine before or after showering.
Inspect the skin over the bunion daily for redness, blistering, or skin breakdown, especially in patients with diabetes.
Track pain levels with a 0-10 scale weekly; escalate to podiatric review if pain increases consistently despite footwear optimization.
prognosis: Bunions are progressive deformities; they do not resolve without intervention and tend to worsen over years, particularly if narrow footwear use continues. Conservative treatment controls symptoms effectively in mild-to-moderate deformity in the majority of patients, but does not reverse the bony deformity. After surgical correction, over 85% of patients report satisfactory outcomes and lasting pain relief. Patient-reported outcome measures (MOXFQ, FAAM) show clinically significant improvement in 80-90% at 2-year follow-up across osteotomy techniques. Recurrence after surgery occurs in 10-25% at 5-10 years depending on surgical technique, footwear compliance, and underlying biomechanical factors; Lapidus arthrodesis has the lowest long-term recurrence rate. Women who return to narrow footwear postoperatively have substantially higher recurrence rates. Second-toe deformity often improves spontaneously after first-toe realignment when treated early.
recoveryTimeline: Non-surgical: symptom relief achievable within 4-8 weeks of footwear change and orthotic fitting. Surgical: return to postoperative shoe at 2 weeks; to normal footwear at 6-10 weeks (distal osteotomy) or 8-12 weeks (Lapidus); full return to sport and fitness activities at 3-6 months for osteotomy and 6-12 months for arthrodesis.
complications: Transfer metatarsalgia under the second and third metatarsals from first-ray load reduction — typically managed with metatarsal pad and orthotic adjustment; may require surgical metatarsal shortening osteotomy if severe.
Avascular necrosis of the first metatarsal head after distal osteotomy, occurring in less than 1% of Chevron osteotomies; risk increased with excessive lateral translation.
Hallux varus (over-correction of the deformity producing medial deviation of the toe) — requires revision surgery in approximately 2-5% of cases.
Persistent stiffness and reduced first MTP dorsiflexion after surgery, particularly relevant for runners; addressed with physiotherapy and staged mobilization.
Radiographic recurrence of deformity (HVA returning toward preoperative levels), occurring in 10-25% at 5-10 years; most do not require revision surgery if symptoms are mild.
Wound infection or delayed healing at surgical incision site, occurring in 1-3% of forefoot procedures; risk elevated in diabetes and peripheral vascular disease.

supportResources.name: American Podiatric Medical Association — Bunions
supportResources.description: Patient-facing information on bunion causes, treatment options, and finding a podiatrist.

supportResources.name: American Orthopaedic Foot and Ankle Society — Hallux Valgus
supportResources.description: Orthopedic surgeon perspective on surgical options, recovery, and expected outcomes.

supportResources.name: NHS UK — Bunions
supportResources.description: UK National Health Service patient guide covering diagnosis, self-management, and surgical referral criteria.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gout
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Gout produces acute attacks of severe MTP joint pain with redness and swelling that resolve completely between episodes; bunion pain is chronic and persistent without acute attack pattern. Gout is confirmed by serum urate and synovial fluid crystals; bunion is confirmed by weight-bearing radiograph showing angular deformity.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hallux Rigidus (First MTP Osteoarthritis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hallux rigidus is osteoarthritis of the first MTP joint causing dorsal osteophyte formation and restricted dorsiflexion. The toe remains straight (no valgus angulation), and the prominent bony lump is dorsal (top of foot) rather than medial. Both may coexist in advanced bunion disease.

confusedWithConditions.name: Metatarsalgia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Metatarsalgia causes plantar forefoot pain under the lesser metatarsal heads without a medial bony prominence. It can coexist with bunion as a secondary effect (transfer loading) but may also occur independently from tight footwear, weight, or Morton's neuroma.

relatedConditions.name: Flat Feet
relatedConditions.relevance: Pes planus and first-ray hypermobility are the leading biomechanical drivers of bunion progression; treating flat feet may slow bunion advancement.

relatedConditions.name: Hammer Toe
relatedConditions.relevance: Second-toe hammer deformity is a direct consequence of first-toe lateral displacement crowding the adjacent digit.

relatedConditions.name: Metatarsalgia
relatedConditions.relevance: Transfer loading from a dysfunctional first ray causes pain under the second and third metatarsal heads, which frequently accompanies bunion.

relatedConditions.name: Gout
relatedConditions.relevance: Both affect the first MTP joint; chronic gout can accelerate arthritic change at the bunion joint and cause diagnostic confusion.

relatedConditions.name: Fungal Toenail Infection
relatedConditions.relevance: Nail dystrophy from onychomycosis is common in the same foot affected by bunion, and active fungal infection can complicate surgical wound healing.

faqs.question: What causes a bunion on your foot?
faqs.answer: Bunions are caused by a combination of inherited foot structure (flat feet, hypermobile first ray, round metatarsal head) and external pressure from narrow or high-heeled footwear. Family history is present in about 68% of cases. Footwear alone does not cause bunions, but it accelerates deformity in genetically predisposed feet.

faqs.question: Can a bunion go away without surgery?
faqs.answer: The bony deformity of a bunion does not resolve without surgery. Conservative treatment — wide footwear, orthotics, toe spacers, and physiotherapy — controls pain effectively in most mild-to-moderate cases and slows progression, but cannot correct the underlying angular deformity. Surgery is required to correct the alignment permanently.

faqs.question: How do I know if my bunion needs surgery?
faqs.answer: Surgery is considered when bunion pain persists despite 3-6 months of correct footwear, orthotics, and anti-inflammatory treatment, or when deformity is severe (hallux valgus angle above 40 degrees), the big toe overlaps the second toe, or significant arthritic change is present on X-ray. A podiatrist or foot surgeon can assess severity.

faqs.question: How long is recovery after bunion surgery?
faqs.answer: Recovery varies by procedure. After Chevron distal osteotomy (the most common technique), patients walk in a protective boot from day one and return to normal shoes at 6-10 weeks. Lapidus fusion requires 6-8 weeks non-weight-bearing. Most patients return to sport and fitness at 3-6 months for osteotomy and up to 12 months after arthrodesis.

faqs.question: Do bunions come back after surgery?
faqs.answer: Radiographic recurrence of hallux valgus occurs in 10-25% of patients at 5-10 years, depending on the technique and footwear choices after surgery. Lapidus arthrodesis has the lowest recurrence rate. Wearing narrow or high-heeled footwear postoperatively significantly increases recurrence risk.

faqs.question: What is the best footwear for bunions?
faqs.answer: Shoes with a wide, straight toe box at least as wide as the metatarsal heads, a low heel (under 2.5 cm), a firm midsole, and a flexible forefoot are best. Leather or stretchy mesh uppers accommodate the bunion prominence. Custom orthotics inside supportive footwear address flat-foot drivers of the deformity.

faqs.question: Are bunion splints effective?
faqs.answer: Night splints and toe spacers reduce pressure on the bunion and may improve comfort, but randomized trials show they do not correct the angular deformity. They are best used to prevent symptoms during sleep and reduce daytime pain, rather than as a curative device. Footwear change and orthotics provide more functional benefit.

faqs.question: Can bunions cause problems with other toes?
faqs.answer: Yes. As the big toe drifts laterally, it crowds the second toe and disrupts the plantar plate of the second MTP joint, causing hammer-toe deformity and crossover-toe instability. First-ray dysfunction also transfers excess load under the second and third metatarsal heads, producing metatarsalgia and plantar calluses.

faqs.question: Is hallux valgus the same as a bunion?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hallux valgus is the medical term for the angular deformity in which the big toe drifts toward the second toe. The bunion is the bony bump on the inner foot that results from the exposed metatarsal head. The two terms describe the same condition from different perspectives.

faqs.question: What exercises help bunion pain?
faqs.answer: Abductor hallucis strengthening exercises — toe-spread and hold, towel scrunches, short-foot exercise — improve dynamic first-ray stability and reduce pain in mild hallux valgus. An RCT showed 3-5 degrees of angular improvement after 8 weeks of daily intrinsic foot exercises. Calf stretching and arch massage are useful adjuncts.

faqs.question: Can children get bunions?
faqs.answer: Yes. Juvenile hallux valgus develops before skeletal maturity and accounts for 3-6% of cases. It tends to be bilateral and familial, associated with flat feet or generalized ligament laxity. Surgical correction is generally deferred until the growth plates close to reduce recurrence risk; orthotics and footwear guidance are used in the meantime.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a bunion and gout?
faqs.answer: Gout causes acute episodes of severe first MTP joint pain, redness, and swelling that resolve completely between attacks, triggered by high blood uric acid. A bunion produces a persistent bony prominence with chronic, pressure-related pain. Weight-bearing X-ray shows angular deformity for bunion; blood urate and joint aspiration diagnose gout.

faqs.question: Can I walk after bunion surgery?
faqs.answer: Yes, for most procedures. After Chevron osteotomy or MICA surgery, patients walk in a flat postoperative shoe from day one. After Lapidus arthrodesis, non-weight-bearing with crutches or a knee scooter is required for 6-8 weeks. Full, unrestricted walking typically resumes at 8-12 weeks post-surgery.

faqs.question: Will orthotics fix a bunion?
faqs.answer: Orthotics do not reverse the bony deformity of a bunion. They address the biomechanical contributors — flat feet, first-ray hypermobility — to reduce ongoing deforming forces and redistribute plantar load. Studies show orthotics improve pain by 20-30% versus footwear change alone, making them a valuable part of conservative management.

faqs.question: Is bunion surgery painful?
faqs.answer: Surgery is performed under regional ankle block or general anesthesia, so the procedure is painless. Postoperative pain is managed with elevation, ice, and oral analgesics. Most patients report moderate discomfort for 1-2 weeks, reducing to mild aching by weeks 3-4. Nerve sensitivity over the surgical site can persist for 3-6 months.

metaDescription: Bunion (hallux valgus): a bony bump at the base of the big toe causing pain and deformity. Symptoms, causes, diagnosis and treatment options reviewed here.

sources.title: Nix S, Smith M, Vicenzino B. 2010. Prevalence of hallux valgus in the general population: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Journal of Foot and Ankle Research 3:21.
Pique-Vidal C, Sole MT, Antich J. 2007. Hallux valgus inheritance: pedigree research in 350 patients with bunion deformity. Journal of Foot and Ankle Surgery 46(3):149-154.
Valisena S, Petri GJ, Ferrero A. 2017. Treatment of hallux valgus: a systematic review. La Chirurgia degli Organi di Movimento 99(1):101-115.
Jain S, Mannan K. 2013. The diagnosis and management of hallux valgus. BMJ 347:f4800.
